behaviors.description: A request whose signature does not match its body is rejected with a 401 response; the body is not forwarded.
behaviors.justification: Needs an external error-path HTTP contract check (`401`) plus verification that no enqueue side effect occurred. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly requires the HTTP error response shape, which triggers the System exception.
behaviors.test_description: Send a real HTTP webhook request with an invalid `X-Signature-256` to a running service instance; assert status is `401` and verify via queue readback/inspection that no event was persisted.

behaviors.description: An event is written to the persistent queue before the HTTP 200 acknowledgment is returned to the sender.
behaviors.justification: Requires proving ordering between an external response and a persistent side effect via round-trip observation (System triad #3), and it also references HTTP 200 behavior. I considered Integration, but rejected it because this ordering guarantee is externally visible only with real round-trip timing/readback.
behaviors.test_description: Issue a real HTTP request and instrument/observe timestamps around queue persistence and response completion; assert queue write is committed before the `200` response is observed by the client.

behaviors.description: The handler returns the HTTP response without waiting for downstream processing of the event to finish.
behaviors.justification: Core risk is async handoff/orchestration across local modules (handler, queue, processor). No real OS/network/subprocess requirement is explicit, and no AI dependency exists. I considered System, but rejected it because the behavior text does not require validating a specific external response contract shape beyond non-blocking control flow.
behaviors.test_description: Wire handler + queue + downstream processor in-process; make downstream processing intentionally slow and assert response completion occurs before processor completion signal.

behaviors.description: The response arrives within 3 seconds wall-clock time even when downstream processing of earlier events is slow and concurrent.
behaviors.justification: This is a real-time concurrency SLA (`within 3 seconds wall-clock`) under load-like conditions; mocked timing and in-process shortcuts can hide scheduler/event-loop/backpressure effects. I considered Integration, but rejected it because the behavior text explicitly requires wall-clock latency under concurrency, which is an externally observed runtime property.
behaviors.test_description: Run the service and generate concurrent webhook traffic while earlier events are processed slowly; measure end-to-end client-observed response latency and assert each targeted response is <=3s.

behaviors.description: An event whose ID was already received is not written to the queue a second time.
behaviors.justification: Failure appears at module handoff/state level (dedupe check plus queue write). Local DB/filesystem-backed persistence is enough to catch this without requiring real external network/process boundaries. I considered System, but rejected it because the behavior text does not require external HTTP/error contract validation or external side-effect proof beyond local persistence semantics.
behaviors.test_description: Submit two events with the same ID through the handler stack using real local persistence; assert only one queue record exists for that ID.

behaviors.description: When the queue reports it is full, the handler returns 503 with a Retry-After header.
behaviors.justification: Explicit external error-path contract check is required (`503` + `Retry-After` header), which triggers System triad #2. I considered Integration, but rejected it because status/header contract verification is explicitly part of the behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Force the queue-full condition against a running service, send a real HTTP request, and assert response status is `503` and `Retry-After` header is present and valid.

behaviors.description: The persistent queue actually enforces its configured capacity limit and rejects events that would exceed it.
behaviors.justification: This is primarily queue/storage enforcement logic across components and persistence; a real local backend (DB/filesystem) provides sufficient reality signal. I considered System, but rejected it because behavior text does not require external HTTP/API contract checks or cross-process/network effects.
behaviors.test_description: Configure queue capacity to a small value, write up to the limit, then attempt one more enqueue and assert deterministic rejection plus unchanged persisted count.
